sloka_sanskrit_iast: Karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana | Mā karma-phala-hetur bhūr mā te saṅgo 'stvakarmaṇi ||
translation_english: You have a right to perform your duty, but not to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself the cause of the results, and never be attached to inaction.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Dhyāyato viṣayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate | Saṅgāt sañjāyate kāmaḥ kāmāt krodho 'bhijāyate || Krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ | Smṛti-bhraṁśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati ||
translation_english: While contemplating the objects of the senses, attachment arises. From attachment comes desire, from desire anger, from anger delusion, loss of memory, destruction of discernment, and ruin.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt | Sva-dharme nidhanaṁ śreyaḥ para-dharmo bhayāvahaḥ ||
translation_english: It is better to perform one's own duty imperfectly than another's duty perfectly. Another's duty leads to fear.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet | Ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ ||
translation_english: One should elevate oneself by the mind, not degrade oneself. The mind alone is the friend and the enemy of the self.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yasmān nodvijate loko lokān nodvijate ca yaḥ | Harṣāmara-bhayodvegair mukto yaḥ sa ca me priyaḥ ||
translation_english: One who is not disturbed by the world and who does not disturb the world, who is free from joy, envy, fear, and anger, is dear to Me.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Dehino 'smin yathā dehe kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā | Tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati ||
translation_english: Just as the embodied soul passes from childhood to youth to old age, so too it passes into another body. The wise are not deluded by this.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Understanding impermanence reduces grief and confusion.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ | Ubhayor api dṛṣṭo 'ntas tv anayos tattva-darśibhiḥ ||
translation_english: The unreal has no existence; the real never ceases to be. The truth about both has been seen by the seers of reality.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Clarity arises from distinguishing the real from the transient.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Veda-vināśinaṁ nityaṁ ya enam ajam avyayam | Kathaṁ sa puruṣaḥ pārtha kaṁ ghātayati hanti kam ||
translation_english: One who knows the self to be indestructible, eternal, unborn, and immutable—how can such a person kill or cause another to be killed?

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Knowledge of the self alters perception of action and consequence.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ | Vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthita-dhīr munir ucyate ||
translation_english: One who is not disturbed by distress, who does not hanker after happiness, and who is free from attachment, fear, and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Inner steadiness is marked by freedom from agitation caused by pleasure and pain.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yadā saṁharate cāyaṁ kūrmo 'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ | Indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā ||
translation_english: One who withdraws the senses from sense objects, as a tortoise withdraws its limbs into the shell, is firmly fixed in wisdom.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Wisdom involves conscious withdrawal from overwhelming sensory engagement.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Mayi sarvāṇi karmāṇi sannyasyādhyātma-cetasā | Nirāśīr nirmamo bhūtvā yudhyasva vigata-jvaraḥ ||
translation_english: Dedicate all actions to Me, with your mind focused on the Self, free from expectation and possessiveness, and fight without inner agitation.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Action performed without inner agitation is emphasized over withdrawal.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ | Sa buddhimān manuṣyeṣu sa yuktaḥ kṛtsna-karma-kṛt ||
translation_english: One who sees inaction in action and action in inaction is wise among humans and is engaged in all actions with understanding.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: True understanding of action transcends surface appearances.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Tad viddhi praṇipātena paripraśnena sevayā | Upadekṣyanti te jñānaṁ jñāninas tattva-darśinaḥ ||
translation_english: Learn the truth by approaching a spiritual teacher with humility, inquiry, and service. The wise who have seen the truth will instruct you.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: True knowledge arises through humility and inquiry.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Naiva kiñcit karomīti yukto manyeta tattva-vit | Paśyañ śṛṇvan spṛśañ jighrann aśnan gacchan svapan śvasan ||
translation_english: One who knows the truth thinks, 'I do nothing at all,' even while seeing, hearing, touching, smelling, eating, moving, sleeping, and breathing.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Freedom arises from seeing oneself as the witness, not the doer.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Na prahṛṣyet priyaṁ prāpya nodvijet prāpya cāpriyam | Sthira-buddhir asammūḍho brahma-vid brahmaṇi sthitaḥ ||
translation_english: One who neither rejoices upon obtaining the pleasant nor laments upon encountering the unpleasant is steady in understanding and established in wisdom.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Inner balance reflects clarity of understanding.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Taṁ vidyād duḥkha-saṁyoga-viyogaṁ yoga-saṁjñitam | Sa niścayena yoktavyo yogo 'nirviṇṇa-cetasā ||
translation_english: Know that state to be yoga which is the separation from union with suffering. This yoga should be practiced with determination and a steady mind.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Yoga is inner freedom, not escape.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yato yato niścarati manaś cañcalam asthiram | Tatas tato niyamyaitad ātmany eva vaśaṁ nayet ||
translation_english: Wherever the restless and unsteady mind wanders, one should gently bring it back under the control of the Self.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Mental steadiness develops through patient redirection, not force.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Manuṣyāṇāṁ sahasreṣu kaścid yatati siddhaye | Yatatām api siddhānāṁ kaścin māṁ vetti tattvataḥ ||
translation_english: Among thousands of people, one strives for perfection, and among those who strive, hardly one truly knows Me.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: True understanding requires sustained effort.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu mām anusmara yudhya ca | Mayy arpita-mano-buddhir mām evaiṣyasy asaṁśayaḥ ||
translation_english: Therefore, remember Me at all times and perform your duty. With mind and intellect fixed on Me, you will surely attain Me.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Remembrance harmonizes action and devotion.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Ananyāś cintayanto māṁ ye janāḥ paryupāsate | Teṣāṁ nityābhiyuktānāṁ yoga-kṣemaṁ vahāmy aham ||
translation_english: To those who are constantly devoted to Me and who worship Me with exclusive focus, I provide what they lack and preserve what they have.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Single-minded devotion removes anxiety about outcomes.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Ahaṁ sarvasya prabhavo mattaḥ sarvaṁ pravartate | Iti matvā bhajante māṁ budhā bhāva-samanvitāḥ ||
translation_english: I am the source of all spiritual and material worlds. Knowing this, the wise worship Me with devotion.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Wisdom culminates in reverent understanding.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Kālo 'smi loka-kṣaya-kṛt pravṛddho lokān samāhartum iha pravṛttaḥ |
translation_english: I am Time, the great destroyer of worlds, engaged here in annihilating all beings.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Confrontation with impermanence dissolves ego.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānāṁ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca | Nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ sama-duḥkha-sukhaḥ kṣamī ||
translation_english: One who is free from hatred, friendly and compassionate to all beings, free from possessiveness and ego, balanced in pleasure and pain, and forgiving—such a devotee is dear to Me.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Devotion expresses itself through inner balance and compassion.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Amanitvam adambhitvam ahiṁsā kṣāntir ārjavam ...
translation_english: Humility, unpretentiousness, non-violence, forgiveness, simplicity, and steady pursuit of knowledge—this is declared to be wisdom.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Wisdom expresses itself as character, not intellect alone.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Prakāśaṁ ca pravṛttiṁ ca moham eva ca pāṇḍava | Na dveṣṭi sampravṛttāni na nivṛttāni kāṅkṣati ||
translation_english: One who neither hates illumination, activity, and delusion when they arise, nor longs for them when they cease, transcends the modes.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Freedom lies in non-reactivity toward inner states.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Mamaivāṁśo jīva-loke jīva-bhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ | Manaḥ-ṣaṣṭhānīndriyāṇi prakṛti-sthāni karṣati ||
translation_english: The living beings in this conditioned world are My eternal fragments. Due to conditioned life, they struggle with the six senses, including the mind.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Inner struggle arises from identification with the senses.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Abhayaṁ sattva-saṁśuddhir jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ | Dānaṁ damaś ca yajñaś ca svādhyāyas tapa ārjavam ||
translation_english: Fearlessness, purity of heart, cultivation of knowledge, self-control, sacrifice, study, and simplicity—these are divine qualities.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Inner virtues form the foundation of spiritual life.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Manaḥ-prasādaḥ saumyatvaṁ maunam ātma-vinigrahaḥ | Bhāva-saṁśuddhir ity etat tapo mānasam ucyate ||
translation_english: Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, self-control, and purity of emotion—this is called mental austerity.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Mental refinement is an inner discipline.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Adhiṣṭhānaṁ tathā kartā karaṇaṁ ca pṛthag-vidham | Vividhāś ca pṛthak ceṣṭā daivaṁ caivātra pañcamam ||
translation_english: The body, the doer, the instruments, the different kinds of effort, and divine will—these are the five factors of action.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Action arises from multiple causes, not ego alone.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Mukta-saṅgo 'nahaṁ-vādī dhṛty-utsāha-samanvitaḥ | Siddhy-asiddhyor nirvikāraḥ kartā sāttvika ucyate ||
translation_english: One who is free from attachment and ego, endowed with determination and enthusiasm, and unaffected by success or failure, is called a doer in the mode of goodness.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Pure action is free from ego and reactivity.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Cetasā sarva-karmāṇi mayi sannyasya mat-paraḥ | Buddhi-yogam upāśritya mac-cittaḥ satataṁ bhava ||
translation_english: Mentally surrender all actions to Me, be devoted to Me, take refuge in discernment, and remain conscious of Me always.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Inner surrender harmonizes action and wisdom.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Dṛṣṭvemāṁ sva-janaṁ kṛṣṇa yuyutsuṁ samupasthitam | Sīdanti mama gātrāṇi mukhaṁ ca pariśuṣyati || Vepathuś ca śarīre me roma-harṣaś ca jāyate | Gāṇḍīvaṁ sraṁsate hastāt tvak caiva paridahyate ||
translation_english: Seeing my own people, O Krishna, arrayed and eager to fight, my limbs fail and my mouth dries. My body quivers, my hair stands on end, my bow slips from my hand, and my skin burns.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Arjuna's grief is the entry point for all spiritual inquiry — crisis can be a catalyst for wisdom.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Ya enaṁ vetti hantāraṁ yaś cainaṁ manyate hatam | Ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate || Na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṁ bhūtvā bhavitā vā na bhūyaḥ | Ajo nityaḥ śāśvato 'yaṁ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||
translation_english: One who thinks the Self slays and one who thinks the Self is slain — both are mistaken. The Self neither slays nor is slain. The Self is never born, never dies. It is unborn, eternal, ever-existing, and primeval. It is not slain when the body is killed.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Grief dissolves when one understands the eternal, indestructible nature of the Self.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Āpūryamāṇam acala-pratiṣṭhaṁ samudram āpaḥ praviśanti yadvat | Tadvat kāmā yaṁ praviśanti sarve sa śāntim āpnoti na kāma-kāmī ||
translation_english: As rivers flow into the ocean, which remains undisturbed despite being ever full, so all desires enter the person of steady wisdom, and such a person attains peace — not the one who hankers after desires.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Peace comes not from suppressing desires but from a settled inner ocean that receives all without being disturbed.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ | Ahaṅkāra-vimūḍhātmā kartāham iti manyate ||
translation_english: All actions are carried out in every way by the modes of nature. One deluded by ego thinks, 'I am the doer.'

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The sense of 'I am the doer' is ego's illusion; nature acts through us when we are identified with the body-mind.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Indriyāṇi parāṇy āhur indriyebhyaḥ paraṁ manaḥ | Manasas tu parā buddhir yo buddheḥ paratas tu saḥ ||
translation_english: The senses are superior to the gross body; the mind is superior to the senses; the intellect is superior to the mind; and the Self is superior to the intellect.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Understanding the hierarchy of the self helps one identify where to direct attention to overcome desire.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata | Abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛjāmy aham || Paritrāṇāya sādhūnāṁ vināśāya ca duṣkṛtām | Dharma-saṁsthāpanārthāya sambhavāmi yuge yuge ||
translation_english: Whenever there is a decline of righteousness and an increase of unrighteousness, O Arjuna, I manifest Myself. To protect the righteous, to annihilate the wicked, and to reestablish the principles of dharma, I appear age after age.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The cosmic principle of dharma is self-correcting; divine force restores balance whenever it is severely disrupted.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Na hi jñānena sadṛśaṁ pavitram iha vidyate | Tat svayaṁ yoga-saṁsiddhaḥ kālena ātmani vindati ||
translation_english: In this world, there is no purifier equal to knowledge. One perfected in yoga discovers this within oneself in time.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Self-knowledge is the ultimate purifier because it dissolves the root cause of all entanglement.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ | Lipyate na sa pāpena padma-patram ivāmbhasā ||
translation_english: One who performs action surrendering it to the divine, abandoning attachment, is not tainted by sin — just as a lotus leaf is not wetted by water.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The lotus metaphor captures how action without attachment remains pure — engaged in the world but not bound by it.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Cañcalaṁ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham | Tasyāhaṁ nigrahaṁ manye vāyor iva suduṣkaram || Asaṁśayaṁ mahā-bāho mano durnigrahaṁ calam | Abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate ||
translation_english: The mind is restless, turbulent, obstinate, and very strong, O Krishna. I think it is as difficult to control as the wind. Without doubt, O mighty-armed one, the mind is difficult to control — but it can be brought under control through practice and detachment.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Arjuna voices the universal struggle with the restless mind; Krishna affirms the challenge but offers a concrete path: practice and non-attachment.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Daivī hy eṣā guṇa-mayī mama māyā duratyayā | Mām eva ye prapadyante māyām etāṁ taranti te ||
translation_english: This divine illusion of Mine, composed of the three modes of nature, is very difficult to overcome. But those who surrender to Me can cross beyond this Maya.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Maya is not an obstacle to be destroyed by force but a veil to be dissolved through surrender and grace.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yaṁ yaṁ vāpi smaran bhāvaṁ tyajaty ante kalevaram | Taṁ tam evaiti kaunteya sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ ||
translation_english: Whatever state of being one remembers when departing from the body at the time of death, O son of Kunti, that state one attains — always absorbed in that being.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The quality of consciousness at death reflects a lifetime of inner cultivation; therefore, living mindfully is the preparation.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Yat karoṣi yad aśnāsi yaj juhoṣi dadāsi yat | Yat tapasyasi kaunteya tat kuruṣva mad-arpaṇam ||
translation_english: Whatever you do, whatever you eat, whatever you offer in sacrifice, whatever you give, whatever austerity you perform — do that as an offering to Me.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: All of life becomes a sacred offering when infused with the spirit of devotion — this is the path of karma-bhakti synthesis.

supportive_practices: dedicating daily work to the divine

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Aham ātmā guḍākeśa sarva-bhūtāśaya-sthitaḥ | Aham ādiś ca madhyaṁ ca bhūtānām anta eva ca ||
translation_english: I am the Self, O Gudakesha, seated in the hearts of all beings. I am the beginning, middle, and end of all beings.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The divine is not distant — it is the very Self within the heart of every being.

image_tags: Krishna as Self in all beings

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Na tu māṁ śakyase draṣṭum anenaiva sva-cakṣuṣā | Divyaṁ dadāmi te cakṣuḥ paśya me yogam aiśvaram ||
translation_english: But you cannot see Me with your physical eyes. Therefore I give you divine eyes. Behold My supreme mystic power.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Seeing the divine requires a transformed perception — grace opens what ordinary sight cannot reach.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Ye tu sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi sannyasya mat-parāḥ | Ananyenaiva yogena māṁ dhyāyanta upāsate || Teṣām ahaṁ samuddhartā mṛtyu-saṁsāra-sāgarāt | Bhavāmi na cirāt pārtha mayy āveśita-cetasām ||
translation_english: But those who worship Me by surrendering all their activities, being devoted to Me with exclusive focus, meditating on Me — for them I am the swift savior from the ocean of birth and death, O Partha.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: For those who struggle with abstract philosophy, surrendered devotion is an accessible and complete path to liberation.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Samaṁ sarveṣu bhūteṣu tiṣṭhantaṁ parameśvaram | Vinaśyatsv avinaśyantaṁ yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati ||
translation_english: One who sees the Supreme Lord dwelling equally in all beings — the imperishable within the perishable — truly sees.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Genuine spiritual vision sees the one imperishable Self equally in all forms — this is the foundation of compassion.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Māṁ ca yo 'vyabhicāreṇa bhakti-yogena sevate | Sa guṇān samatītyaitān brahma-bhūyāya kalpate ||
translation_english: One who serves Me with unwavering devotion through bhakti-yoga transcends these three modes of nature and reaches the level of Brahman.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Unwavering bhakti is not merely emotional sentiment but a complete yoga that lifts one beyond the conditioning of the three gunas.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Sarvasya cāhaṁ hṛdi sanniviṣṭo mattaḥ smṛtir jñānam apohanaṁ ca | Vedaiś ca sarvair aham eva vedyo vedānta-kṛd veda-vid eva cāham ||
translation_english: I am seated in the hearts of all beings. From Me come memory, knowledge, and forgetfulness. I am to be known through all the Vedas; I am the compiler of Vedanta and the knower of the Vedas.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The divine pervades all cognition — memory, understanding, and forgetting alike arise from and return to this presence within.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Tejaḥ kṣamā dhṛtiḥ śaucam adroho nāti-mānitā | Bhavanti sampadaṁ daivīm abhijātasya bhārata ||
translation_english: Spiritual power, forgiveness, fortitude, purity, freedom from malice, and absence of excessive pride — these are the qualities of one born with divine endowment.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: Divine qualities are cultivable character traits — forgiveness and fortitude are as much spiritual practices as meditation.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Sattvānurūpā sarvasya śraddhā bhavati bhārata | Śraddhāmayo 'yaṁ puruṣo yo yac-chraddhaḥ sa eva saḥ ||
translation_english: The faith of each person, O Bharata, is shaped by their nature. A person is constituted by faith — whatever one deeply believes, that is what one becomes.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: We are shaped by what we most fundamentally believe — faith is not blind but an expression of one's deepest inner nature.

sloka_sanskrit_iast: Man-manā bhava mad-bhakto mad-yājī māṁ namaskuru | Mām evaiṣyasi satyaṁ te pratijāne priyo 'si me || Sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṁ śaraṇaṁ vraja | Ahaṁ tvāṁ sarva-pāpebhyo mokṣayiṣyāmi mā śucaḥ ||
translation_english: Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, worship Me, bow down to Me. You will come to Me — truly I promise you, for you are dear to Me. Abandon all varieties of dharma and simply surrender to Me alone. I shall deliver you from all sinful reactions — do not fear.

interpretive_notes.core_teaching: The culminating teaching of the Gita — complete surrender dissolves all fear and all karmic weight; grace is the ultimate refuge.
